metadata.title: NEUROPSYCHOLOGY OF AUTISM IN YOUNG CHILDREN AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR EARLY INTERVENTION
metadata.abstract: Given a variety of encouraging outcome reports regarding the progress of young children with autism in certain early intervention programs, there is a new emphasis nationally on providing more systematic and focused interventions for these children. There is also considerable national debate regarding the appropriate nature and forms of successful interventions. While published intervention strategies differ with respect to world view, beliefs about intervention, and goals for outcome, successful programs produce better outcomes for children because there are deep similarities between them that address core neuropsychological difficulties of young children with autism. These core difficulties affect development of intersubjectivity, imitation/praxis, possible deficits in executive functioning, emotional functioning, and sensory/arousal functioning. The neuropsychological perspective provides a tool for examining any intervention approach to autism to discriminate: what need(s) the approach addresses, what educational strategies it uses to address the need, and the degree to which it fits with the needs of a given child. Successful intervention approaches: 1) address the individual child’s and family’s needs, strengths, and preferences, 2) can be delivered by the persons responsible for the child’s intervention, and 3) result in satisfying gains for the child and family. �[1998 Wiley-Liss, Inc.]

evidence.original_text: Given a variety of encouraging outcome reports regarding the progress of young children with autism in certain early intervention programs, there is a new emphasis nationally on providing more systematic and focused interventions for these children. There is also considerable national debate regarding the appropriate nature and forms of successful interventions.

Given a variety of encouraging outcome reports regarding the progress of young children with autism in certain early intervention programs, there is a new emphasis nationally on providing more systematic and focused interventions for these children.

The neuropsychological perspective provides a tool for examining any intervention approach to autism to discriminate: what need(s) the approach addresses, what educational strategies it uses to address the need, and the degree to which it fits with the needs of a given child.
These core difficulties affect development of intersubjectivity, imitation/praxis, possible deficits in executive functioning, emotional functioning, and sensory/arousal functioning.

Lovaas’s Early Autism Project at UCLA has the strongest scientific evidence for effectiveness of any model thus far [McEachlin et al., 1993; Lovaas, 1987]. The approach uses operant techniques, mostly reinforcement, but also some punishment techniques, to teach a wide range of social, language, cognitive, and self-care skills and to reduce inappropriate behaviors.

entries.normalized: 洛瓦斯早期自闭症项目

evidence.original_text: TEACCH is a well-known intervention approach for people with autism across the life span. Early studies documented the effectiveness of the TEACCH model in improving behavior and learning of children with autism [Schopler et al., 1971; Marcus et al., 1978].

Rogers and colleagues have described the effects of a developmentally oriented, 22-hour per week, centerbased treatment model in Denver that emphasized play, language, cognition, and social relations.

Joint attention refers to the coordination of one’s own attention with a partner’s attention around an object or event—a triangle of attention in which partners are aware that their attention is coordinated with the other’s. Young children with autism have difficulty in all of these areas. The joint attention area has been studied the most thoroughly, with marked deficits demonstrated repeatedly [Mundy et al., 1987, 1990; Landry and Loveland, 1988].

The ability to carry out motor imitations appears to depend on a particular type of motor function, praxis. The term ‘praxis’ refers to a process underlying the planning and execution of novel and complex motor patterns and sequences [Ayres, 1985; Heilman, 1979]. Classic tests of praxis involve motor imitation, pantomime, performing accurate gestures on command, and performing complex motor sequences, like making coffee.

Executive function refers to behaviors that are used to plan, organize, and carry out complex, future-oriented, goaldirected activities. Executive function skills involve setting goals, generating the most efficient behaviors to reach the goal, including novel behaviors, inhibiting competing behaviors, monitoring progress toward the goal, and keeping the goal and plan active in working memory until completion [Pennington and Ozonoff, 1996].

The problem is currently thought to involve working memory—the ability to keep a goal in mind until it is completed and to organize one’s behavior adaptively to accomplish the goal [Bennetto et al., 1996].

evidence.section: Intervention approaches that deal with the emotion difficulties
evidence.original_text: A particularly important part of the Denver Model involves the frequent use of ‘sensory social routines.’ Sensory social routines are repeated dyadic interactions, built on pleasurable activities that have strong sensory, movement, and social foundations.

The neuropsychological perspective provides a tool for examining any intervention approach to autism to discriminate: what need(s) the approach addresses, what educational strategies it uses to address the need, and the degree to which it fits with the needs of a given child.

evidence.section: Intervention approaches to executive function difficulties
evidence.original_text: TEACCH views problems with organization and sequential memory as basic challenges in autism and the TEACCH approach provides maximum scaffolding for executive function problems [Mesibov et al., 1994].
In the Denver Model, a variety of intervention techniques directly address intersubjectivity deficits. First is the emphasis on balancing adult-directed episodes with child-directed episodes.

evidence.section: The Work of O. Ivar Lovaas
evidence.original_text: Outcome effectiveness was examined by Lovaas and colleagues in two different long-term studies [McEachlin et al., 1993; Lovaas, 1987] involving 19 young children with autism treated intensively with behavioral therapy over a two-year span, as compared to two matched comparison groups.

Ozonoff and Cathcart [1998] recently examined the effects of adding TEACCH interventions for a half-hour per day in the homes of 11 preschoolers also enrolled in other intervention programs. This was a controlled study involving a waiting list design to match a comparison group on age, severity of autism, and developmental level pretreatment.

evidence.section: The Work of O. Ivar Lovaas
evidence.original_text: The approach uses operant techniques, mostly reinforcement, but also some punishment techniques, to teach a wide range of social, language, cognitive, and self-care skills and to reduce inappropriate behaviors.

evidence.section: Intervention approaches to executive function difficulties
evidence.original_text: Schedules help executive function problems with initiative, inhibition, and sequencing by telling the person when to start, when to stop, and what the steps are in a task.
Intentional communications are fostered through the use of joint activity routines and a variety of communication temptations [Prizant and Wetherby, 1987].

evidence.section: The Work of O. Ivar Lovaas
evidence.original_text: Outcome effectiveness was examined by Lovaas and colleagues in two different long-term studies [McEachlin et al., 1993; Lovaas, 1987] involving 19 young children with autism treated intensively with behavioral therapy over a two-year span, as compared to two matched comparison groups. Outcome variables were obtained at two points in time—after first grade and again in early adolescence.

Ozonoff and Cathcart [1998] recently examined the effects of adding TEACCH interventions for a half-hour per day in the homes of 11 preschoolers also enrolled in other intervention programs. This was a controlled study involving a waiting list design to match a comparison group on age, severity of autism, and developmental level pretreatment.

evidence.section: The Work of O. Ivar Lovaas
evidence.original_text: At both follow-up points, mean IQ differences of 25 to 30 points separated the groups (experimental group mean IQ 84.5, control group 54.9 at second follow-up). Similar levels of difference were reported for all adaptive behavior scores at the second follow-up. There were significant differences in educational functioning, with 47% of the experimental group functioning well in regular classrooms without special supports, whereas only 0–2% of controls were thus placed.

Outcomes from the three studies appear similar. Of the 34 children with autism receiving intensive treatment across the three studies, virtually all continued to demonstrate symptoms of autism after treatment. One study reports a posttreatment 25-point IQ difference between treated and untreated children, with a mean (typically nonverbal) IQ of 90 for treated children [Sheinkopf and Siegel, 1998]. Ratings of symptom severity decreased across treated children in all three studies.

Their findings involved significantly greater gains for the children receiving TEACCH interventions for 4 months. Gains were widespread, involving imitation, fine and gross motor development, cognitive skills, and overall developmental level as measured on the PEP-R.

While published intervention strategies differ with respect to world view, beliefs about intervention, and goals for outcome, successful programs produce better outcomes for children because there are deep similarities between them that address core neuropsychological difficulties of young children with autism.

evidence.section: NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL PROFILE OF AUTISM IN YOUNG CHILDREN
evidence.original_text: Examining autism at the neuropsychological level may also provide some direction for intervention. In the same way that understanding the neuropsychological deficit in dyslexia as an impairment of phonological processing of language led to the development of effective interventions for children with dyslexia, directing interventions in autism at the neuropsychological level may improve their efficacy.

While the methodologies involved do not allow for causal statements to be made, these studies provide converging support for the hypothesis that well-run early intervention programs for young children with autism, when carried out for a significant number of hours per week under rigorous levels of structure and instruction, and with very low student:teacher ratios, result in developmental acceleration for the children and reduction in autistic symptomatology [Dawson and Osterling, 1997].

evidence.section: The Work of O. Ivar Lovaas
evidence.original_text: The main methodological weakness involves lack of random assignment to groups. Other criticisms include lack of documentation of treatment hours, limitations of the outcome measures used, and the issue of full recovery vs. continuing symptoms of highfunctioning autism [Foxx, 1993; Mesibov, 1993; Mundy, 1993; Gresham and MacMillan, 1997].

While none has the methodological strengths of Lovaas’s studies, two studies involved control groups and all report pre- and posttreatment data taken roughly two years after the beginning of intervention.

Given the empirical designs used and absence of independent replications, the field awaits empirical validation of any comprehensive treatments for early autism [Rogers, 1998]. Lovaas’s data aside, other published studies report similar outcome data.
